title: Laminectomy -- Introduction
content: Posterior spinal decompression is one of the most common surgical procedures to release neural structures when nonoperative treatment has failed and is usually the procedure performed for degenerative conditions such as spinal stenosis, especially in middle-aged and elderly patients. [1] It is one of the most common spinal surgeries among cohorts more than 65 years of age. The classical laminectomy constitutes a central, facet joint sparing laminectomy. The spinous process and the lamina are removed limited laterally to the medial part of facet joints. There was no benefit in preserving the midline structures. [2] The central canal, the lateral recesses, and the neural foramina need to be decompressed for good clinical recovery and prevention of failed back surgery syndrome.

title: Lumbar Degenerative Disk Disease -- Treatment / Management
content: Lumbar discectomy with fusion: The Swedish Lumbar Spine Study Group has reported systematic evidence on the superiority of outcomes of fusion for degenerative lumbar disc disease when compared with nonsurgical treatment. [12] Fusion has been considered for a long time as the gold standard for the management of lumbar degenerative disc disease. [13] Lumbar total disc replacement: [14] [15] The main indication is a single-level disc disease with disease-free facet joints. In comparison with fusion, this has been reported to have better patient outcomes and lower rates of adjacent segment disease. On the other side, postoperatively, patients may still complain of persistent back pain, which could be due to the subtle instability of the prosthesis. Dislocation of the polyethylene inlay has been reported as a complication as well. This can be treated by either revision arthroplasty or conversion to arthrodesis.

title: Anatomy, Back, Lumbar Spine -- Surgical Considerations
content: Lumbar decompression is a technique used to relieve pain caused by a compressed nerve root(s). Two of the most common types of nerve root compression are lumbar central canal stenosis and lumbar radiculopathy, reviewed in the next section. Lumbar decompression comes in several forms, including microdiscectomy, foraminotomy, laminotomy, laminectomy, or a combination of these. Laminectomy (surgical removal of the laminae) is considered the standard surgical treatment for the management of lumbar spinal stenosis. If the decompression, as mentioned above, leads to iatrogenic instability, then a fusion is also performed. [10]

title: Cervical Spondylosis -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical
content: The posterior approach options consist of partial discectomy, laminotomy-foraminotomy, laminoplasty, and laminectomy. Foraminotomy alone is adequate in patients with foraminal stenosis due to bone spur formation and/or lateral disc herniation. Laminectomy allows for multilevel cervical decompression, and it is typically paired with instrumented fusion to prevent postoperative iatrogenic kyphosis. Laminoplasty offers a motion-preserving alternative, but it should be selected only after confirming the absence of significant kyphosis, segmental instability, or substantial ventral compressive pathology, as these findings contraindicate the procedure.

title: Cervical Myelopathy -- Treatment / Management
content: Laminoplasty, whether in the “open-door” or “French-door” configuration, offers indirect decompression by hinging and expanding the lamina while preserving the posterior elements and motion. In multilevel stenosis without significant preoperative kyphosis, laminoplasty yields neurological improvement rates comparable to laminectomy. However, it may not adequately relieve severe axial neck pain, and is contraindicated when preoperative kyphosis exceeds approximately 10° or in the presence of instability. Performing dome laminotomies at the laminae adjacent to the laminoplasty directly decompresses and prevents static or dynamic spinal cord kinking that can occur when the cord dorsally migrates after decompression. [40]

title: Spinal Cord Compression -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Approaches
content: Despite the above approaches, posterior approaches remain the most common, especially in the lumbar spine. This is due to the following general reasons: ease of access, decreased morbidity, often shorter patient recovery, and versatility for the surgeon. For most degenerative lumbar pathology, a posterior approach is a good option. For lumbar claudication secondary to congenital stenosis, a simple laminectomy may be adequate. If significant disc pathology is also present, a laminectomy may be supplemented with a partial or complete discectomy. Complete discectomies may be accomplished through a posterior corridor (posterior lumbar interbody fusion [PLIF]) or a more lateral corridor (transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion [TLIF]) and need to be supplemented with disc grafts as well as pedicle screw fixation. [34] [35]

title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 944
content: Laminectomy and intervertebral disc excision are spinal surgical procedures performed to relieve compression of neural elements and to treat disc pathology. These interventions are directly linked to the development of post‑laminectomy syndrome (متلازمة ما بعد استئصال الصفيحة الفقرية), a condition in which patients continue to experience persistent back pain, radicular symptoms, or functional limitation after the surgery. Recognizing this relationship is important for clinicians, as it underscores the need for careful patient selection, thorough pre‑operative counseling, and postoperative strategies—such as multimodal pain management, rehabilitation, and, when appropriate, additional interventions—to mitigate the risk and impact of post‑laminectomy syndrome on patient outcomes.

title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502
content: Excision is classified as a clinical action within the broader group “Clinical actions A‑G” and serves as an umbrella term for a wide range of operative procedures. It includes adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, or dorsally, with or without biopsy and with possible removal of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—as well as several biliary surgeries such as cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, and associated cholangiography, which can be performed via open or laparoscopic techniques. Craniectomy or craniotomy performed exploratively in the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region also falls under Excision, as do various parathyroidectomy approaches—including cervical, sternal‑split, and transthoracic routes—each potentially combined with mediastinal exploration. In spinal surgery, Excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and excision of herniated intervertebral disc at a single lumbar level, but explicitly excludes laminectomy procedures that involve only exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for spinal stenosis across cervical and thoracic segments (both 1‑2 vertebral levels and more extensive segments). Additionally, Excision covers “other or therapeutic procedures on nose, mouth and pharynx” and is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, highlighting its relevance to both general surgical and specialized reconstructive interventions. This comprehensive definition clarifies the scope of Excision, distinguishing it from specific laminectomy techniques while integrating diverse surgical modalities under a single procedural category.

content: Excision is defined as a therapeutic category that encompasses a variety of surgical methods while specifically excluding certain spinal decompression techniques. The procedures excluded from the Excision category include laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord and/or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for spinal stenosis when performed on one or two vertebral segments in the cervical spine, on lumbar segments (except for spondylolisthesis), on more than two cervical vertebral segments, and on more than two thoracic vertebral segments. Conversely, Excision includes a broad range of operative methods. These methods comprise cholecystectomy with exploration of the common bile duct and cholangiography, cholecystectomy with common duct exploration combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty (with or without cholangiography), choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage, or calculus removal (with or without cholecystotomy) and possible transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the supratentorial region, suboccipital craniectomy for exploration or decompression of cranial nerves, laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common duct exploration, and various forms of parathyroidectomy—including standard parathyroidectomy, parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via cervical, sternal‑split, or transthoracic approaches. In addition to these operative techniques, the Excision category is linked to procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, indicating that excisional methods may also be applied in aesthetic or reconstructive nasal surgery. This integrated view clarifies which surgical interventions are classified under Excision, which are deliberately excluded, and how the category relates to both abdominal and cranial procedures as well as to facial plastic surgery.

content: Exploration procedures encompass a broad range of operative techniques in which surgeons directly examine an anatomic region to diagnose, treat, or remove pathology. The category includes adrenalectomy—partial or complete removal of the adrenal gland performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, with or without biopsy—and may also involve excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. In hepatobiliary surgery, cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, with or without cholangiography or choledochoenterostomy, is classified as an exploration method, as is choledochotomy or choledochostomy performed for ductal exploration, drainage, or stone removal, sometimes accompanied by transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty. Neurosurgical exploration is represented by craniectomy or craniotomy performed for supratentorial investigation and suboccipital craniectomy aimed at exploring or decompressing cranial nerves. Spinal exploration procedures consist of laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy; these are specified for one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis), and thoracic regions, and for more than two thoracic segments, with the latter variations noted as possibly included methods. Urologic and endocrine exploration includes radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration and parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration. The broader classification “Clinical actions A‑G” serves as a parent category for exploration procedures, indicating that these diverse surgical methods are organized under a common clinical action framework.
